title: Thread pool rewrite
body:
This PR introduces a simpler approach to the implementation of `V3ThreadPool` and multi-threading in general.

## Issues with the current thread pool

The current thread pool is more generic than it needs to be, which brings unnecessary complexity and debugging difficulty.

For one, it can execute scheduled jobs at any time in the background, outside of `MT_DISABLED` functions and `V3ThreadPool::requestExclusiveAccess` scopes. Of course, `V3ThreadPool::waitForFutures` is used for awaiting scheduled jobs, but it can be omitted. `MT_DISABLED` and `V3ThreadPool::requestExclusiveAccess` were introduced as guarantees that there are no background jobs. For such regions, the thread pool has to handle a stop request, to ensure that no jobs are running at the moment. In this stopped state, the thread pool executes a job in "exclusive access" mode and then resumes stopped jobs. Unfortunately, the implementation of this functionality requires an additional mutex and complex logic for handling such requests in various possible states.

The `V3ThreadPool::resize` function stands out as overly complex, as it allows for changing the number of workers at runtime. This is currently not being used, and we don't foresee it ever being used. The only cases we need is starting `n` workers, and then waiting for `n` workers to finish.

Another issue is handling errors/exiting. There is complex logic for handling errors, errors during errors, fatal errors, etc. All this to avoid ugly abort messages and display a proper error message to the user.

This complexity resulted in hard to solve bugs. One of the problems involved deadlocks in `~V3ThreadPool` ([#4672](https://github.com/verilator/verilator/pull/4672)). Another was a spurious lock failure in `~V3ThreadPool` ([#4938](https://github.com/verilator/verilator/pull/4938)). The most recent one was a locked `V3ThreadPool` destruction issue, solved with a workaround ([#5040]( 
https://github.com/verilator/verilator/pull/5040)). It is likely that more bugs will follow.

## Overview of the new thread pool

The idea behind the new thread pool is to remove all features that are not used by Verilator and tailor it to the specific problem we're solving. Currently, `V3ThreadPool` is only used in `V3EmitC::emitcImp` for emitting functions for each `AstNodeModule`.

`V3ThreadPool` remains a global object, but its management strategy changed; it is managed via `V3ThreadScope` object. This ensures that multi-threading is limited to certain scopes. It is guaranteed that during the creation and after the destruction of `V3ThreadScope` the thread pool is in a clean state (there are no running jobs).

Considering that there are only select places where the `V3ThreadPool` will be used, it would be easier to tag those rather than the inverse. That's the second reason why `V3ThreadScope` has been introduced. This renders `V3MtDisabledLockGuard` and similar `MtDisabled` constructs redundant.

Futures are also removed, since jobs in `V3EmitC::emitcImp` are not expected to return any value. Calling `V3ThreadScope::wait` explicitly or `V3ThreadScope` destructor implicitly is sufficient for ensuring their completion. If a need for futures arises, they can be added back.

The `V3ThreadPool::resize` method has been removed since, as mentioned, it was only used once in the initialization process when the number of workers was known. However, Verilator does not change available number of workers in runtime, therefore dynamic worker allocation is not needed.

### Types and key functions

- **`V3ThreadPool`** - thread pool implementation intended to be operated by `ThreadScope`. Stored in `v3Global`. Not intended to be used directly.

- **`V3ThreadScope`** - public interface to `V3ThreadPool` for scheduling jobs. Waits for all running jobs in destructor (or in `wait()`, see below). Using this class, we bind parallel job execution to the scope where `V3ThreadScope` is declared. Additionally, this mechanism assures that `ThreadPool` is in clean state (without running jobs) after each stage where this `ThreadScope` is used.

  - **`V3ThreadScope::enqueue`** - schedules job in the global `ThreadPool` instance.
  - **`V3ThreadScope::wait`** - waits until all jobs running in the global `ThreadPool` are finished. A manual alternative to `V3ThreadScope`'s destructor.
  
### Error handling

In the face of a job error, `_exit` is called (`std::abort` in the debug build). `V3ThreadPool` is leaked, thus no stop nor shutdown requests need to be sent. This prevents deadlocks with the `V3Error` and `V3ThreadPool` mutexes or any possible future locks. In fact, no global destructors are called, which avoids accessing invalid global state from still-running workers. There is also no need for dealing with complex error states in `~V3ThreadPool`. Resource cleanup is left to the OS. As for flushing streams, the one we care about in case of an error is stderr. Stderr is not buffered, so the user still gets the full error messsage.    

### Thread safety

We tested the thread pool by continuously running verilation of large designs for about a week which resulted in no deadlocks or failures.

We implemented a model of the thread pool in the [Spin model checker](https://github.com/nimble-code/Spin). The model was proved correct.

The new implementation is still checked by Clang's thread safety analysis:
- `V3ThreadScope` is guarded by `MtScopeMutex` which ensures that only one instance of it exists in a given scope. This prevents misuse of the thread pool by constraining management of it to one `V3ThreadScope` instance at the time.

- `V3ThreadPool` mutex is guarded by Clang's thread safety measures, similarly as in the previous implementation.
